stem: Let \( \mathbf{v}_1 = (1,\ [[s]],\ [[t]]) \), \( \mathbf{v}_2 = ([[p]],\ [[m22]],\ [[m23]]) \), and \( \mathbf{v}_3 = ([[q]],\ [[m32]],\ [[m33]]) \). What is the dimension of \( \operatorname{span}\{\mathbf{v}_1, \mathbf{v}_2, \mathbf{v}_3\} \)?
solution: The dimension of the span equals the rank of the matrix whose rows are \( \mathbf{v}_1, \mathbf{v}_2, \mathbf{v}_3 \). Row-reducing gives rank \( [[answer]] \), so \( \dim(\operatorname{span}\{\mathbf{v}_1,\mathbf{v}_2,\mathbf{v}_3\}) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This subtracts the dimension from the ambient dimension \(3\) instead of reporting it directly.
This overcounts the number of independent vectors by one.
This undercounts the number of independent vectors by one.
This overcounts the number of independent vectors by two, which cannot exceed the number of vectors given.
This assumes all three vectors are the zero vector.
This assumes the three vectors are automatically independent without checking.

stem: Let \( \mathbf{v}_1 = (1,\ [[s]],\ [[t]]) \), \( \mathbf{v}_2 = ([[p]],\ [[m22]],\ [[m23]]) \), \( \mathbf{v}_3 = ([[q]],\ [[m32]],\ [[m33]]) \), and \( \mathbf{v}_4 = ([[v4a]],\ [[v4b]],\ [[v4c]]) \), four vectors in \( \mathbb{R}^3 \). What is the dimension of \( \operatorname{span}\{\mathbf{v}_1, \mathbf{v}_2, \mathbf{v}_3, \mathbf{v}_4\} \)?
solution: Four vectors in \( \mathbb{R}^3 \) can span at most a \(3\)-dimensional space. The dimension of the span equals the rank of the \(4\times3\) matrix formed by the vectors, which is \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This subtracts the dimension from the number of vectors \(4\) instead of reporting the dimension directly.
This overcounts the dimension of the span by one.
This undercounts the dimension of the span by one.
This overcounts the dimension of the span by two.
This assumes all four vectors are the zero vector.
This assumes the span is automatically all of \( \mathbb{R}^3 \) without checking for dependence.

stem: Let \( \mathbf{v}_1 = (1,\ [[s1]],\ [[s2]],\ [[s3]]) \), \( \mathbf{v}_2 = ([[r2_1]],\ [[r2_2]],\ [[r2_3]],\ [[r2_4]]) \), and \( \mathbf{v}_3 = ([[r3_1]],\ [[r3_2]],\ [[r3_3]],\ [[r3_4]]) \), three vectors in \( \mathbb{R}^4 \). What is the dimension of the subspace \( \operatorname{span}\{\mathbf{v}_1, \mathbf{v}_2, \mathbf{v}_3\} \)?
solution: Three vectors can never span more than a \(3\)-dimensional subspace, even inside \( \mathbb{R}^4 \). The dimension of the span equals the rank of the \(3\times4\) matrix formed by the vectors, which is \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This wrongly assumes the vectors could span all of the \(4\)-dimensional ambient space.
This overcounts the dimension of the span by one.
This undercounts the dimension of the span by one.
This overcounts the dimension of the span, which cannot exceed the number of vectors given.
This assumes all three vectors are the zero vector.
This assumes the vectors are automatically independent without checking.

stem: Let \( \mathbf{v}_1 = (1,\ [[a]],\ [[b]]) \), \( \mathbf{v}_2 = ([[c]],\ [[d]],\ [[e]]) \), and \( \mathbf{v}_3 = ([[f]],\ [[g]],\ k) \), where \( k \) is a real parameter. For what value of \( k \) is \( \dim(\operatorname{span}\{\mathbf{v}_1,\mathbf{v}_2,\mathbf{v}_3\}) \) less than \( 3 \) (i.e., the three vectors fail to be a basis of \( \mathbb{R}^3 \))?
solution: The dimension of the span drops below \(3\) exactly when \( \det\begin{bmatrix} 1 & [[a]] & [[b]] \\ [[c]] & [[d]] & [[e]] \\ [[f]] & [[g]] & k \end{bmatrix} = 0 \). Expanding along the first row gives a linear equation in \( k \), whose solution is \( k = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the negative of the correct value, from a sign error in the cofactor expansion.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.
This mistakes the given entry \( g \) for the critical value of \( k \).
This mistakes the given entry \( f \) for the critical value of \( k \).
